name: Hip Dysplasia
description: Abnormal development of the hip joint causing laxity, pain, and degenerative joint disease. The femoral head does not fit properly into the acetabulum, leading to cartilage wear, osteoarthritis, and lameness. Larger breeds are most affected. Screening is via standardized radiographs evaluated by OFA or PennHIP.

name: Elbow Dysplasia
description: A group of developmental abnormalities affecting the elbow joint including fragmented medial coronoid process (FCP), osteochondritis dissecans (OCD), and ununited anconeal process (UAP). Causes forelimb lameness, pain, and secondary osteoarthritis. Affected dogs should not be bred.

name: Brachycephalic Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
description: A combination of anatomical abnormalities in flat-faced breeds including stenotic nares (pinched nostrils), elongated soft palate, everted laryngeal saccules, and hypoplastic trachea. Causes chronic respiratory distress, exercise intolerance, overheating, and can be life-threatening. Severity is graded 0-3 via functional testing.

name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
description: Degeneration of the intervertebral discs causing herniation into the spinal canal, compressing the spinal cord. In chondrodystrophic breeds (French Bulldog, Dachshund), Type I Hansen herniations are acute and severe. Can cause pain, paralysis, and incontinence. Surgical intervention is often required.
severity_scale: Grade 1 (pain only) - Grade 5 (paralysis with no deep pain)

name: Hemivertebrae
description: A congenital malformation of the vertebrae where vertebral bodies are wedge-shaped rather than rectangular, causing spinal curvature (kyphosis/scoliosis). Common in screw-tailed breeds like French Bulldogs. Severe cases cause spinal cord compression and neurological deficits.

name: Patellar Luxation
description: Dislocation of the kneecap (patella) from its normal groove. Can be medial or lateral. Small and toy breeds are most commonly affected. Graded 1-4 based on severity. Chronic luxation leads to cartilage erosion and osteoarthritis.

name: Skin Fold Dermatitis (Intertrigo)
description: Inflammation and infection of skin folds due to trapped moisture, friction, and bacterial/yeast overgrowth. Breeds with facial wrinkles, tail folds, and body folds (French Bulldog, Pug, Shar-Pei) are highly predisposed. Requires daily cleaning and antimicrobial therapy when active.

name: Cherry Eye (Prolapsed Nictitans Gland)
description: Prolapse of the third eyelid (nictitating membrane) gland, appearing as a red, swollen mass at the inner corner of the eye. Common in brachycephalic breeds and Cocker Spaniels. The exposed gland is prone to inflammation and reduced tear production if not surgically corrected.

name: Corneal Ulcers
description: Erosion or open sore on the cornea surface. Brachycephalic breeds with prominent eyes (French Bulldog, Pug) are at high risk due to reduced corneal sensitivity, poor blink coverage, and exposure. Can progress to corneal rupture if untreated.

name: Distichiasis
description: Abnormal growth of eyelashes from the meibomian glands along the eyelid margin, causing irritation to the cornea. Affected dogs may show no symptoms or develop chronic eye irritation and corneal damage depending on lash stiffness and direction.

name: Cataracts
description: Opacification of the eye lens causing reduced vision or blindness. Can be hereditary (juvenile/early onset) or age-related. Multiple breeds affected. Progressive and irreversible without surgical lens replacement. OFA eye certification exams screen for inherited cataracts.

name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
description: A group of inherited disorders causing gradual degeneration of retinal photoreceptor cells, leading to night blindness progressing to total blindness. There is no treatment. DNA testing can identify carriers. Multiple forms exist (prcd-PRA, GR-PRA1, GR-PRA2) affecting different breeds.

name: Mitral Valve Disease (MVD)
description: Degenerative thickening and prolapse of the mitral valve leaflets causing incomplete closure and backward blood flow (regurgitation). The most common acquired heart disease in dogs. Cavalier King Charles Spaniels are uniquely affected: nearly 100% by age 10, with early onset (50% by age 5). Leads to congestive heart failure if unmanaged.
severity_scale: Stage A (at risk) - Stage D (refractory heart failure) per ACVIM

name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
description: A disease of the heart muscle causing ventricular dilation and reduced contractility. Leads to congestive heart failure, arrhythmias, and sudden death. Large and giant breeds (Doberman, Great Dane, Boxer) are predisposed. Nutritional DCM linked to grain-free diets is also reported.

name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
description: Thickening of the left ventricular heart muscle without dilation, reducing chamber volume and impairing relaxation. The most common heart disease in cats. Ragdolls and Maine Coons carry breed-specific mutations (MYBPC3 gene). Can cause sudden death, thromboembolism, or congestive heart failure. Progression is unpredictable.
severity_scale: Mild (asymptomatic, mild hypertrophy) - Severe (heart failure, thromboembolism)

name: Syringomyelia (SM)
description: A severe neurological condition where fluid-filled cavities (syrinxes) form within the spinal cord, most commonly in the cervical region. Strongly associated with Chiari-like Malformation (skull too small for brain). Cavalier King Charles Spaniels are overwhelmingly predisposed. Causes intense neuropathic pain — patients may scream when touched near the neck.
severity_scale: Asymptomatic (incidental finding) - Severe (debilitating pain, neurological deficits)

name: Deafness (Congenital Sensorineural)
description: Hereditary deafness caused by degeneration of cochlear blood supply shortly after birth, linked to the piebald/merle gene affecting melanocyte migration in the inner ear. Affects predominantly white-coated and merle-patterned dogs. Can be unilateral or bilateral.

name: Hypothyroidism
description: Insufficient production of thyroid hormones (T3/T4) due to lymphocytic thyroiditis or idiopathic atrophy. Common in medium to large breeds. Causes metabolic slowdown affecting multiple body systems: skin, coat, weight, and energy levels. Lifelong hormone replacement therapy required.
diagnosis_methods: OFA thyroid panel (T4, free T4, TSH, TgAA)

name: Allergic Dermatitis (Atopy)
description: Chronic allergic skin disease triggered by environmental allergens (pollen, dust mites, molds) or food. Results in intense itching, recurrent skin infections, and ear inflammation. French Bulldogs and Golden Retrievers are commonly affected. Requires lifelong management with avoidance, medications, or immunotherapy.

name: Cancer (Hemangiosarcoma)
description: An aggressive malignant tumor of blood vessel lining cells, most commonly arising in the spleen, heart (right atrium), or liver. Golden Retrievers and German Shepherds are overrepresented. Often silent until acute internal bleeding occurs. Prognosis is poor even with surgery and chemotherapy.

name: Cancer (Lymphoma)
description: Cancer of lymphocytes and lymphoid tissues. The most common hematopoietic malignancy in dogs. Golden Retrievers have a significantly elevated lifetime risk (1 in 8). Can affect lymph nodes, spleen, liver, bone marrow, or extranodal sites. Multicentric form is most common.
severity_scale: Stage I (single node) - Stage V (bone marrow/blood involvement)

name: Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (GDV/Bloat)
description: Life-threatening condition where the stomach fills with gas and twists on its axis, cutting off blood supply. Deep-chested large breeds (Golden Retriever, Great Dane) are at highest risk. Requires emergency surgery. Prophylactic gastropexy is recommended for at-risk breeds during spay/neuter.

name: Polycystic Kidney Disease (PKD)
description: Hereditary condition causing multiple fluid-filled cysts to form in the kidneys, progressively replacing functional tissue. Most commonly seen in Persian and exotic shorthair cats. Autosomal dominant inheritance — a single copy of the mutated gene causes disease. Progresses to chronic kidney failure.
diagnosis_methods: genetic DNA testing for PKD1 mutation
severity_scale: Few cysts (preserved function) - End-stage renal failure

name: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
description: An inherited neurodegenerative disorder causing progressive loss of spinal cord motor neurons, leading to muscle weakness and atrophy. Maine Coon cats carry a breed-specific autosomal recessive mutation. Onset in kittens (3-4 months). Non-painful but progressive; affected cats have reduced mobility and lifespan.
diagnosis_methods: genetic DNA testing for SMA mutation

name: Neonatal Isoerythrolysis (NI)
description: A life-threatening condition in newborn kittens where maternal antibodies in colostrum destroy the kitten's red blood cells due to blood type incompatibility (Type B queen nursing Type A or AB kittens). Ragdolls have a high prevalence of Type B blood. Kittens fade rapidly and die within days if not removed from the queen.
diagnosis_methods: blood typing of queen and stud before breeding
severity_scale: Preventable (if typed before breeding) - Fatal if not caught

name: Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD)
description: A syndrome encompassing various conditions causing bladder and urethra inflammation in cats. Maine Coons are predisposed to idiopathic cystitis and urethral obstruction. Stress, obesity, and diet are key risk factors. Male cats are at risk of complete urethral blockage — a life-threatening emergency.
